transcript: Hey everybody. How's your day been? Everybody had a good time? All right. Well, I am happy to be here with you today uh to talk about the most secure and the easiest to use way to be secure in the entire Solana ecosystem, Ledger. Let me see if I can get my notes on the slide and the answer is no, but I have them here, so don't worry. Um, so... I want to be on this slide. So, as you guys already know, uh you could say this year will be the worst year for cybercrime every year for the rest of your life and you would be right without needing to do any fact-checking. Um, it's because we get more and more value in our ecosystem every day and it becomes a a bigger and bigger target. And also as you probably know just a few days ago, uh Upbeat, I'm sorry Upbit suffered a 37 million dollar hack uh on Solana assets. And they had to halt withdrawals and, you know, we all know the importance, but we don't always think about the importance of security. But the fact of the matter is is that your phone and your laptop were designed to share information, not to protect value. Um, and so using them to protect value is is ultimately a fool's errand. You're using you're using something for something which it wasn't designed to do. Um, Ledger has been around for 11 years. It was the first and is still the best, um the only uh operating system that's connected to uh an entire suite of products so you can do anything you want to with any of your assets from the security of self-custody and built ground up to do that. But just as a little reminder, you know, what do you need to be secure? Like what does it mean to be secure and why can't a phone or a computer do this kind of architecturally? Well, the the three things that you need, you know, first of all, you need to have um a secure element that generates your private key and stores it. You know, the secure element has special, you know, entropy, etcetera, things that you need to be secure. Um, then you need your cryptographic operations to happen inside of that secure element. Um, you know, not in a general purpose chip, you know, that's that's used for other things. It needs to happen inside of that that secure element. And then third, you need to be able to eyeball with your eyes those transactions on a screen that you can trust. Um, you know, the magic trick of a Ledger device is that it's the same chip that's in your credit card um or your passport, the secure element that, you know, banks and governments rely on, but it's actually powering the screen, right? So there's nothing between the secure element and and your eyes. And that's a that's the thing that your phone is incapable of doing. The fact of the matter is, if you don't have a secure screen, you don't have security, right? So that means even if your private keys are stored offline in a screenless device, at the time of transaction, you are still reliant on the screen on your phone to tell you the truth. And you can't trust that what you see on on your phone is the truth. There's simply too many layers of software in there to trust it. So, why are we here? Because we love Solana. And we love Solana at Ledger too, and I want to talk specifically about what Ledger is doing in the Solana ecosystem today. So, um earlier this year uh at Accelerate, you you may have seen us there announcing the Solana Edition Ledger Flex, which I have which I have here, and we have them at the Ledger booth outside as well. Um, you can you can buy these at the booth here or on ledger.com, and these come with something else that we announced this year. This is the Ledger Recovery Key. This is another secure chip inside this little piece of plastic. It's got NFC and you can electronically back up, it's an electronic PIN-protected backup of your recovery phrase. So super easy, simple, PIN-protected recovery using uh, you know, using a secure chip on both sides to do the magic. So check it out, try it out, and you can grab a a Solana Edition Flex at the booth. Sorry to be grabbing my phone but my my notes are not here. Uh... Um, so let's talk about what we've done with Solana this year because really like I said, you know, Ledger has the the the only, you know, operating system where you can put arbitrary apps uh on the Ledger device, developer.ledger.com if anybody wants to see uh how you build those. Um, but we we uh we've done a lot with the with the um Solana ecosystem this year. The biggest thing is supporting SPL and supporting SPL swaps. So you can now store any of your Solana assets uh with with and protect them with your Ledger. Uh, you can swap, you can do, you know, anything you want. We're constantly updating them um because, you know, more are added all, you know, every day and and we're we're, you know, we're keeping up with uh with what you all are doing. Um, and you can do all of that uh from your Ledger and that's a big step forward. But security is not only for individuals, right? It's also uh for enterprises. So Ledger Enterprise, you may not know that we have an enterprise product, um but hopefully if you're an enterprise you do know that Ledger offers not only Ledger Enterprise but now Ledger Multi-Sig. Um, and our enterprise product also now supports SPL. So if if you are, you know, a uh, you know, a um a a uh a project, a DAO, uh an organization, a um uh an exchange, you can you can use Ledger Enterprise and protect your your SPL that way. I was going to add one more thing. Ah, okay. I understand what happened. I'm also going to announce that there we have at the booth, please come and see us. Uh, we have uh we are announcing today uh also a Bonk Edition Ledger device. So we have a Bonk Flex and a and a Bonk S Plus. And and they're and they're both, you come see them at the device and you can get them on ledger.com. So if you're curious, go to ledger.com. I understand now there was a slide but this is a an earlier edition of that of that presentation. So, um the swap experience I talked through and I really just want to say thank you to all of you for supporting Ledger, for uh for paying attention to my presentation. Um, and remember, you know, at Ledger we never compromise on either security or self-custody. And really you shouldn't either. I I get the question, I was, you know, in interviews today like, at what point, you know, should someone choose security? And that that's such a crazy question to me. Like at what point should you choose security? I mean, at the beginning. If you if you have value um that is, you know, more than than the cost of a Ledger device, you should invest in your security and grab a Ledger. So come on over and see us at the Ledger booth, grab a Solana Edition Ledger Flex. Thanks everybody for attention and I'll see you soon.

# Ledger says popular chip used in smartphones vulnerable to unstoppable attack
Ledger says it was able to gain “full and absolute control” over a smartphone by using electromagnetic pulses to take over its chip.
Listen0:00
![Ledger says popular chip used in smartphones vulnerable to unstoppable attack](https://images.cointelegraph.com/cdn-cgi/image/format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=90,width=1434/https://s3.cointelegraph.com/uploads/2025-12/019ae762-6ceb-7bc0-b5a9-b1d764f95489)
News
COINTELEGRAPH IN YOUR SOCIAL FEED
A chip widely used in smartphones has an unfixable vulnerability that could allow attackers to gain complete control and steal private keys stored on the device, according to crypto wallet maker Ledger.
Ledger said in a[report](https://www.ledger.com/blog-is-your-smartphones-hardware-safe)on Wednesday that it tested an attack on the MediaTek Dimensity 7300 (MT6878), and bypassed its security measures to gain “full and absolute control over the smartphone, with no security barrier left standing.”
Ledger security engineers Charles Christen and Léo Benito explained that they took control of the chip using electromagnetic pulses during the chip’s initial boot process.
Crypto wallets often[rely on private keys](https://cointelegraph.com/learn/articles/public-key-vs-private-key), which some users store on their phones, meaning bad actors can extract private keys from a device to[steal from a crypto wallet](https://cointelegraph.com/news/crypto-wallets-becoming-control-centers-digital-lives-podcast).
![Wallet, Cybersecurity, Smartphone, Hacks](https://s3.cointelegraph.com/uploads/2025-12/019ae7d2-04f1-7317-be80-bb71ed9295b6)*Ledger security engineers Charles Christen and Léo Benito used electromagnetic pulses to expose a vulnerability in MediaTek’s Dimensity 7300 chip. Source:*[*Ledger*](https://www.ledger.com/blog-is-your-smartphones-hardware-safe)
“There is simply no way to safely store and use one’s private keys on those devices,” Christen and Benito said.
## Smartphone chip vulnerability can’t be fixed
The fault injection vulnerability can’t be fixed through a software update or patch, because the issue is coded into the silicon of the smartphone’s system on chip (SOC), meaning “users stay vulnerable even if the vulnerability is disclosed,” according to Christen and Benito.
Ultimately, the attack success rate is low, between 0.1% to 1%, but the duo said the speed at which it can be repeatedly initiated means that eventually an attacker will gain access in “only a matter of a few minutes.”
> “Given that we can try to inject a fault every 1 second or so, we repeatedly boot up the device, try to inject the fault, and if the fault does not succeed, we simply power up the SoC and repeat the process.”
## Chipmaker says product isn’t meant for finance
MediaTek told Ledger that electromagnetic fault injection attacks are “out of scope” for the MT6878 chip.
***Related:***[***Cloudflare blames database error for outage that took down 20% of the internet***](https://cointelegraph.com/news/cloudflare-fault-in-bot-system-led-to-outage)
“Like many standard microcontroller circuits, the MT6878 chipset is designed for use in consumer products, not for applications such as finance or HSMs (Hardware Security Modules),” it said.
> “It is not specifically hardened against EMFI hardware physical attacks. For products with higher hardware security requirements, such as hardware crypto wallets, we believe that they should be designed with appro

# Stress-Tested and Validated: How Fireblocks and Solana Handled Crypto’s Largest Liquidation Event
[![Fireblocks Icon](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/fireblocks-logo.svg)
Avi Bashan
Head of R&D, Wallet Solutions
](https://www.fireblocks.com/author/avi-bashan)
4 min. read
![](https://www.fireblocks.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/Solana_Liquidation_Blog-1024x576.webp)
On October 10, 2025,[crypto markets experienced their largest liquidation](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/why-october-2025-down-most-150216105.html)event in history. A whopping $19.5 billion was liquidated across all markets with approximately $1 trillion in total market cap wiped out. Binance halted trading. Ethereum Layer 2s lagged.[Arbitrum fees spiked above $500, with median fees jumping to $116](https://dune.com/ilan_g/oct-10-metrics). The entire ecosystem was under unprecedented stress.
This was the ultimate real-world test of mission-critical infrastructure.
Less than a year after we dramatically improved[Fireblocks’ Solana performance during the Trump meme coin surge](https://www.fireblocks.com/blog/fireblocks-solana-network-performance-optimization), the October 10th liquidation event provided validation of everything we built. Not only did our infrastructure hold, but it excelled, proving that enterprise-grade blockchain operations can handle maximum stress when it matters most.
## When Markets Panic, Infrastructure Must Stay Calm
During the liquidation cascade, Solana processed the chaos without breaking stride. Validators processed[100,000 transactions per second (TPS) of inbound traffic (6x normal peak traffic) while maintaining 400-millisecond block times](https://dune.com/solana_team/october-10-performance-comparison). The network sustained 6,000 raw TPS throughout the event with validators processing transactions with zero payment interruptions.
For Fireblocks customers, the platform handled a 200% increase in Solana traffic with zero customer impact and 100% first-attempt transaction success. This is proof that corporate payments can run on Solana even when markets are in crisis.
## The Numbers That Matter: Digital Asset Infrastructure Performance Under Pressure
Let’s look at what happened to the metrics that matter most during stress events:
* **Transaction Landing Rate: 100%**– No change from baseline. Every transaction succeeded on the first attempt, with no retries needed despite the massive surge in network activity.
* **Broadcasting Speed: 23ms (P50)**– Fireblocks’ median time to broadcast a signed transaction remained unchanged at 23 milliseconds, even as transaction volume spiked 2x. This broadcasting speed also represents a significant improvement from the previous median time of 47ms in March 2025.
* **Time to notify about a completed transaction: 2 seconds**– Consistent throughout the event. Fireblocks’ ability to process and notify about confirmed transactions not only maintained baseline performance during the event, but also showed a 3-second improvement since March 2025.### Transaction Fees: The Real Story
Here’s where Solana’s architecture truly shines. While there was fee variability, median transact

#### Key highlights:

- Ledger reveals chip flaw allowing full device control in minutes
- Unpatchable vulnerability threatens crypto users’ private keys
- Researchers show repeated EM attacks make breaches inevitable

[Hardware wallet](https://coincodex.com/article/23051/best-crypto-hardware-wallets/) manufacturer Ledger [has discovered](https://www.ledger.com/blog-is-your-smartphones-hardware-safe) a critical vulnerability in the MediaTek Dimensity 7300 chip, used in several popular smartphones including the [Solana Seeker](https://coincodex.com/article/63492/solana-phone/), a device marketed specifically to cryptocurrency users. According to Ledger’s research team, attackers can gain “complete and absolute control” over affected devices using electromagnetic pulses.

> What if a hacker could gain total control of your smartphone, not via malware, but the hardware itself?
> The [@DonjonLedger](https://twitter.com/DonjonLedger?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw) discovered a potentially unpatchable flaw impacting MediaTek Dimensity 7300 - a popular Android phone SoC - enabling arbitrary code execution in minutes.… [pic.twitter.com/JFHjc20D6f](https://t.co/JFHjc20D6f)
>
> — Charles Guillemet (@P3b7\_) [December 3, 2025](https://twitter.com/P3b7_/status/1996234336081793434?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw)

Security engineers Charles Christen and Leo Benito demonstrated a real-world attack on the MT6878 variant of the chip, bypassing all built-in security protections. By targeting the early boot stage, they were able to inject faults that compromise the device beyond repair.

_The electromagnetic attack setup used by Ledger._

## Why this vulnerability endangers crypto wallets

This flaw is especially dangerous for cryptocurrency users who store private keys on mobile devices. With full control of the phone, attackers can extract private keys and gain unrestricted access to victims’ wallets.

_“There is simply no way to safely store and use one’s private keys on those devices,” Christen and Benito warned._

Because the vulnerability is embedded directly in the chip’s silicon design, it cannot be fixed with software updates or security patches. Even after public disclosure, affected users remain vulnerable.

### Low probability per attempt, but high risk overall

The success rate of the electromagnetic fault-injection attack ranges from 0.1% to 1% per attempt. However, repeated retries make the attack essentially inevitable.

“ _We can try to inject a fault every 1 second or so (we repeatedly boot up the device, try to inject the fault, and if the fault does not succeed, we simply power up the SoC and repeat the process)_” _,_ the researchers explained.

With this method, a successful breach may take only a few minutes.

## MediaTek’s response and broader security implications

MediaTek [_responded_](https://www.ledger.com/blog-is-your-smartphones-har

editorial.headline: Ledger Unveils Bonk Edition Hardware, Cites $37M Upbit Hack as Validation for Self-Custody
editorial.standfirst: Following a massive exchange breach on Solana, Ledger reinforces its 'secure screen' thesis with new SPL-native hardware and enterprise-grade asset support.
key_takeaways: New 'Bonk Edition' Ledger Flex and Nano S Plus announced exclusively at Breakpoint.
Full SPL token and swap support now live across Ledger Live and Ledger Enterprise.
Ian Rogers cites the recent $37M Upbit hack as definitive proof that mobile and exchange custody remain fundamentally broken.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
In a keynote delivered just days after the **Upbit** exchange suffered a $37 million breach of Solana assets, Ledger Chief Experience Officer Ian Rogers wasted no time connecting the dots between institutional failure and hardware necessity. The timing of the hack—which froze withdrawals and rattled the ecosystem—served as a grim backdrop to Ledger’s primary thesis: general-purpose devices and centralized exchanges are architecturally incapable of securing value. Rogers argued that smartphones and laptops, designed fundamentally for information sharing rather than asset protection, leave a "blind spot" between the processor and the user's display that software cannot bridge.

This security philosophy has now been fully integrated into the Solana ecosystem. Rogers announced the immediate availability of **Bonk Edition** devices—specifically the Ledger Flex and Nano S Plus—signaling a deeper alignment with Solana’s retail culture. However, the more significant update for institutional participants is the comprehensive rollout of **SPL token** support across the entire Ledger stack, including **Ledger Enterprise**. This update allows DAOs, exchanges, and funds to manage, swap, and secure Solana-native assets (including SPL swaps) within a fortress-grade environment, moving beyond simple SOL custody.

Rogers’ critique of mobile security was particularly pointed, likely referencing Ledger’s recent disclosure regarding vulnerabilities in **MediaTek** chipsets used in consumer devices. He emphasized that without a "secure screen"—a display directly powered by a secure element, as found in Ledger devices—users are essentially flying blind, trusting a compromised operating system to display transaction truths. "If you don't have a secure screen, you don't have security," Rogers stated, dismantling the narrative that mobile-first wallets can ever match the assurance of a dedicated hardware signer.

The session concluded with a clear message to the room of developers and VCs: as value on Solana grows, so does the target on its back. With the **Ledger Recovery Key** now offering an electronic, PIN-protected backup solution and the **Solana Edition Ledger Flex** bridging the gap between usability and security, Ledger is positioning itself not just as a wallet manufacturer, but as the requisite infrastructure layer for anyone—individual or institution—serious about retaining their Alpha.
